videos.transcript: What's up guys! We're here in Marrakech, Morocco, and in this video we're going to show you the best things to do in and around the city. We're also going to sprinkle in some travel tips to help keep you safe and have the best trip possible. So hope you enjoyed the video! The bustling city of Marrakech is located in the center of Morocco. With its vibrant colors, buzzing atmosphere, mix of tradition and modernity, and aromatic smells, it's a city like no other. Marrakech truly stimulates all of your senses at once. In this video, we'll highlight the best things to do in and around this UNESCO World Heritage City. Now let's dive in! We're Zac and Ine. We make helpful videos about destinations around the world so you can easily plan your own adventures. The medina is the old city of Marrakech, entirely encircled by light pink walls. It's the heart of the city, packed with shops, traditional houses called "riads", restaurants, and many of the historic sites. To learn all about the medina and the local life in Marrakech, we recommend going on a walking tour of the city on your first day here. It's a great way to become familiar with the labyrinth of alleyways as well as the local customs. The tour also takes you to corners that you likely wouldn't be able to find yourself, such as the workshops of the souks. Find a link to the tour that we did in the description below. One of the nice things about the city is that it's pretty compact, it's easy to get around places by walking. But you should be careful crossing the streets because even if there's a crosswalk it's not guaranteed that these cars are going to stop for you. So wait for your moments and you should be good. Morocco in general is known for its delicious, unique cuisine that you can't find anywhere else in the world. Some staples that you have to try include couscous, meat brochettes, and the most iconic dish of all: tagine, which usually includes some kind of meat like chicken, beef, or lamb that is left to simmer and slow-cooked with herbs, spices, and other ingredients such as dates, potatoes, and vegetables. Most restaurants are located near the main square of Jemaa el-fna and you can find personal recommendations in our blog post. But don't shy away from the street food stalls as they offer a delicious and affordable selection of sandwiches and pastries as well as freshly squeezed juice and more exotic dishes. You can't leave Marrakech without visiting one of its historic sites. The Saadian Tombs are a 16th century burial ground of the Saadian Dynasty which ruled over Marrakech between 1524 and 1668. The mausoleums are beautifully decorated and are set amidst a garden. The tombs were walled up by the following ruler to keep his predecessors out of sight and mind, and was only rediscovered in the early 20th century when aerial photography exposed it. If you think this historic spot has beautiful designs, wait until we show you the palaces in just a moment. Marrakech is known for its beautiful gardens and Majorelle Gardens might be the most famous one. Located in Marrakech's New Town, these lush botanical gardens are filled with cacti, palms, and other colorful trees, offering a true escape from the hustle and bustle of the city. It was originally created by artist Jacques Morelle, who lived in this garden and villa complex. It was later purchased by the famous fashion designers Yves-Saint-Laurent and Pierre Bergé, who restored it. Today, it's open to the public and some of the houses have been converted into museums. The "Mellah" or Jewish quarter in Marrakech is another interesting area to visit. Located near the beautiful palaces that we'll discuss at numbers four and three, this quarter was once home to a sizable Jewish population. And while most of the Jewish residents have now left this area, there is still a number of Jewish places here; including the Synagogue; the Jewish cemetery and the Spice Market. This place will tickle your taste buds with a wide range of Moroccan spices, herbs, and herbal teas. Marrakech is known for its traditional souks, which are basically markets or bazaars. In the past, a souk was an area where merchants passed through the city and traded goods. As such, you can find them in different corners inside and outside of the Medina. What makes the souks in Marrakech remarkable is the wide variety of exotic items that you can buy, such as babouches, lanterns, ceramics, carpets, and leather goods. Most of the popular souks can be found next to the Main Square. We put a graphic here on the screen with the names. We also left a link in the description where you can find directions to each souk. When you walk through the souks you will notice that a lot of the vendors will talk to you, trying to get you into their shop. But if you don't want to buy anything, just say "shukran", thank you, and just walk away. And if you do want to buy something, make sure to bargain because usually the price that they will offer is a little bit higher. So haggle down and make sure that when you pay in the end is comfortable for you. The Saadian ruler Al Mansour who was buried in the Saadian tombs we discussed earlier also had built a grand palace in the 16th century. And although it stands largely in ruins today, it's easy to take a journey back in time and imagine the beauty it once had. Step foot inside and you'll see its grandeur, with mosaic-tiled floors, ruined pavilions, and high enclosed ceilings. Make sure to go to the top of the walls where you can take in the views of the palace from above as well as across the Medina. After El Badi Palace, it might be interesting to see the contrast with the beautiful Bahia Palace, which was built in the late 19th century. The interior decoration combines colorful tiles, painted ceilings, and ornate wrought iron features that showcase a lavish lifestyle. The beautiful courtyards with banana leaf plants and trees are a tranquil break from the buzzing city. If you want a true escape from the noise of the city and would like to experience some of Morocco's finest nature, we recommend going on a day trip to the Ouzoud waterfalls. With a height of 110 meters or 360 feet, these are the tallest waterfalls in the country. A day tour from Marrakech allows you to spend around four hours at the falls, which is plenty of time to hike down to the river, take in the beauty, do a short boat ride to get closer to the falls, and then hike up again. There are many restaurants with a stunning viewpoint as well on your way towards the exit. And as a bonus, you'll also encounter many macaque monkeys who call this place home. We really enjoyed this day tour and we left a link to the one that we joined in the description below. If you have more time to spare than just a day, we also recommend venturing to the High Atlas region and the desert. You'll need at least a few days to really enjoy these unique landscapes and we explain all about it in our full Morocco video. Finally, you can't leave Marrakesh without visiting its beating heart: the Jemaa el-fna Square. It's the hub of entertainment, with a variety of food and juice stalls, musicians, fortune tellers, henna tattoo artists, and snake charmers. The square really comes to life in the evening and can be a bit overwhelming for some people because of its loud noise and large crowds. An evening spent here wandering around is a truly Moroccan experience. To escape the chaos, you can head to one of the rooftop cafes next to the square that offer panoramic views. Don't forget to check out the Koutoubia Mosque, which can be found right next to the square. It's Marrakech's most famous landmark with its large minaret that is visible from every direction. There are a few things to do in Marrakech that we didn't mention and we've included a list of those on the screen here for you. If you're planning your Morocco trip, we recommend keeping things organized with our interactive travel planner. Find a discount code below. We also included important links that you'll need for your Marrakech trip in the description, such as a full travel guide with where to stay and where to eat as well as directions to get to each spot that we mentioned. We'll catch you on the next adventure!

there are trains that will take you between major cities you can also rent a grand taxi that you can negotiate a price for as well we chose to take tours that would get us from one city to another uh even for day trips and that worked out really well inside the Medina you're really going to just be on foot most of the time there are taxis that come into the Medina and then take you to other parts of the city in Fez we found that the petite taxis which are little red taxis are great they're just outside the Blue Gate easy to find in maresh the main squares have a lot of taxis showing up you'll be sitting there eating a meal and you'll just see all the taxis pull up so they're not hard to find if you're interested in going between the Medina and the new city especially in Fez you'll take petite taxis but you'll want to negotiate the price in advance they're typically around 20 Durham I would say when you walk in you'd expect to pay 10 Durham and it's probably an extra Durham for each minute that you're in the vehicle and I was surprised when you were texting me saying that during your taxi ride you weren't alone in the cab right I got in to an empty cab but along the way there were three or four different passengers that got in and got out that were along the path that I was on so don't really expect that there's going to be a meter you want to negotiate in advance because obviously those little detours could add up to a little bit of money we didn't take them but there are two different bus services that offer premium service that you can also take ctm and super tours and they actually have different classes of service on the buses they're very nice convenient we'll put a link to both of them so that you know more about them and as we mentioned earlier do make sure you have small bills or change and the coins are really kind of important when you're going even to a bathroom that may be public there's going to be somebody there that maybe handing out toilet paper you want the small coins like a five or 10 Durham coin 20 Durham coins are also very popular but you may not even want to spend that much when you're at these little tiny tip spots so as much as you can carry the change around you may also want to carry some tissues with you because there aren't always attendance and there isn't always toilet paper in some of the public restrooms in the places that you'll be visiting do take time to appreciate the people and the culture of Morocco it may feel a little bit outside your comfort zone to be walking by the sus and having people constantly ask if you want to buy something or people who are holding out their hand or trying to offer you directions that you didn't ask for at the same time realize that they're really trying to be friendly and helpful yes sure some of them are trying to earn money but for the most part we have found the people of Morocco to be really really lovely people don't expect Morocco to be a budget destination it's not one of the cheapest countries we've visited unfortunately when you want to stay in a nicer Riad or a nicer area of the Medina you're going to pay more and that seem to be where the majority of our costs are we could eat fairly cheaply you know you can go out to dinner and basically pay for two people to have a nice meal meal for under 200 Durham so under $20 but a lot of your living cost for actually being here might be elevated and we did spend 30 and 40 and even $60 or more I think on one of our meals we and drinks was an international restaurant which will cost a bit more than having tajine and just be prepared that although it's budget friendly it's not the cheapest destination we have a whole video coming up about this but don't go on large group tours when you're going through the Sahara we had a private tour and it was wonderful you'll see all the details soon but the people that went on group tours have warned us it wasn't the experience we had and when we talked about ours they were like no that we were rushed we were hurried there was so many people it's like yeah skip those do something special for that short time you have in the Sahara desert but we highly recommend that you go on one of those tours and our video will tell you all of the things that you need to know so that you can set up the best experience we hope you've enjoyed this video and we You can expect a 15 to 25 minute ride depending on distance to your accommodation and traffic. Oh, and cabs can be chaotic. When arriving, you won't find a shortage of taxi cabs waiting for you at the arrival gate. You can take a gamble and land on a great driver, but chances are they might try to pull a fast one on you, especially if it's your first time in Morocco. If you choose this option, be sure to negotiate before getting into the cab. Prices should range around 100 to 150 Moroccan deerhams. from the airport to the medina. We recommend that you avoid the headaches and the stress, especially on your first day in Morocco, and pre-book your ride with your Riad or hotel if possible. It avoids overpaying scams or stressful negotiations. And even better, you can pre-book your transfer with Get Your Guide, thereby avoiding any nasty surprises. Actually, we made the decision to book all our excursions in Morocco with Get Your Guide, and we'll dive deeper into that later on, so stay tuned. Moreover, if you're staying near the Gmail town square, you'll be able to walk to many sites and attractions. Alternatively, you can find taxis for longer distances. You'll want to know that Morocco has two types of taxis. one that's called pity taxi or small taxi which are usually used for short distances and not for carrying luggage pieces of any kind. For these taxis, it's also advisable to ask the driver to activate the meter or pre-negotiate your ride. Alternatively, they also have the regular or large taxis, which you guessed it are for longer distances and for carrying larger pieces of luggage. These rides you'll find near the train or bus stations and will most likely need to negotiate for your ride. Number four, don't bring a drone. Don't bring a drone. I brought my Low Pro camera bag and they had me open it various times to check if I had a drone and they grabbed my Nikon and kept asking me over and over if I had a drone somewhere. on the way into the country and on the way out. So, drones are illegal to bring in Morocco without a very specific government permit. Customs will confiscate drones at the airport and you may not even get them back ever. So, if you have one, leave it at home to avoid the risk. Number five, choosing where to stay, reads or hotels. Choosing where to stay in Marrakesh will make a difference in your experience. Staying inside the medina, which is the old city, is our recommendation for an authentic experience. But depending on your stress level and noise tolerance, it can appear to be more chaotic. Alternatively, you might want to consider staying in Vil Noville, which is the new town, more modern hotels, but a bit further from attractions. If possible, we recommend to stay near the Jima Alfna town square for the most part of your stay, thereby absorbing the city's culture, energy, and authenticity. Then you might want to consider migrating closer to the airport before you fly back home. Especially if you have an early flight back, you might not enjoy navigating your way out of the medina at 6:00 in the morning. Moreover, Riads are traditional Moroccan homes with courtyards. Years ago, Moroccans lived in these Riads which serve to house the growing families. Riads also have an inner courtyard which is where the family members would enjoy some fresh air. We personally enjoyed our stay at Riyad Zarka for various reasons. It was located in the medina but on the edge making it easy to navigate in and out and surprisingly quiet. Plus, we had a great host by the name of Simo who even came to pick us up at the taxi drop off. It's worth noting that cars cannot circulate the Gmail in Fna Town Square after a certain time in the morning. So, Simo's help was most useful because without him, we might not have found our way to the Riad. We booked our stay at the Riadzarka using our hotels.com account and we'll leave our affiliate link in the description and blog for your convenience. We're immensely grateful for those of you who use it as it allows us to continue producing valuable content like this. Additionally, RIADs offer charm and a peaceful retreat. But check if they're easy to find. Some are deep inside the maze. You can always use the large squares like Gmail Nefna as a reference. Pro tip, contact your Riad to arrange a porter for luggage if it's deep inside the medina. Tip number six, bring some cash. Morocco uses deerms. It's a closed currency, which means it's not always available to exchange prior to your trip. Consequently, ATMs are easy to find in Marrakesh, including at the airport. Based on our experience, airports don't always have the best exchange rates. We were told by locals to change our money at the Hotel Ali, located on the edge of Jima Alfna. At some point you'll need deer hems because many places in the medina are cash only like small cafes, suks, taxi rides. And with deer hems, you have more negotiating power. You'll enjoy knowing that many attractions can be paid using cards. We recommend that you pre- purchase your entry tickets before even arriving in Morocco. Here's why. For starters, you'll save Moroccan deer hems, which you'll need at some point. Next, I tried to buy tickets to the Jard Majarel the night before our visit, and their site didn't jive with my credit card as it was asking me further authentication, i.e. I would have had to open my phone by removing the airplane mode and pay extra fees. The other reason is because Jard Majarel and Eve Sanaram Museum are popular attractions and they fill up quickly and they operate by timelines. So when buying a ticket in advance, you're guaranteeing your spot in line. Number seven, get a local eim or SIM card. Heirlow or Holofly are excellent examples. If you watched our WZAT or SOA vlogs, you'll see that we tested both of these platforms. Both operate differently. Aerolo offers you low prices, low data plans, but for longer durations. Contrarily, Holif is a little bit more expensive, but offers you unlimited data for specific duration. So then it's up to you to see which plan suits you best. Ellie will leave these links in our video's description and blog. You might be thinking, sure, Wi-Fi is widespread in reads and cafes, but having data for Google Maps, translation apps, and communication is a lifesaver in Marrakesh. We had a solid 4G connection at the time which allowed us to navigate the winding medas and kept us connected as we traveled by train from Marrakesh to Fez and beyond. Number eight, expect culture shock and embrace it. Marrakesh is intense. Lots of sounds, smells, colors, people approaching you to sell you things. And this is all part of the charm and experience. Our best advice is to stay calm, smile, and politely decline if you're not interested. Learn basic words like shukran, which means no thank you in Arabic. It'll save you a lot of stress. But we usually manage to find people who spoke French or English. Number nine, dress modestly. Even though Marraash is a touristy city, it's still culturally conservative. Based on their culture, both men and women should wear long pants or skirts and cover their shoulders where possible. Despite that, we noticed many individuals with tank tops and shorts, and some even looked uncomfortable with all the stairs they were getting. Ellie researched this topic prior to our visit and purchase clothing which confirms with their culture. Feel free to do the same as it'll make you feel more respectful and comfortable and all the more reason to shop for traditional Moroccan clothing in the Medina. Number 10, plan for Friday closures. Friday is the holy day in Islam. Some attractions, shops, and services may be closed or operate on limited hours, especially during Friday prayer time. [Music] We worked this in our favor by planning excursions outside the city since certain attractions would be closed anyways. Note to self, always double check opening hours if you're planning a major sightseeing on a Friday. Number 11, beware of helpful strangers. It's common for people to offer directions or help and then ask you for a tip. I even heard a story from an acquaintance who fell into this trap. If someone insists on guiding you somewhere, be clear that you don't need their assistance or agree on a price beforehand. And like I mentioned earlier, Heirlow or Holifly will usually work well in the medas, but remember you're connecting to their network, so if it fails, you might be happy to have downloaded Google Maps offline, for example. Tip number 12, negotiate before you buy anything. Taggling is part of their culture. It's not rude, it's expected. Moreover, we heard from locals that it's kind of a game for the merchants. Vendors and the Suks often start with a very high price, sometimes as high as three times the actual value. If you'd like to sharpen up your negotiating skills, Marrakesh is the perfect place to start. But don't worry if you're not an expert. Just smile, counter offer, and don't be afraid to walk away. It's part of the dance. And don't be afraid to lowball and negotiate on your terms. You might also want to calculate your highest acceptable price before you negotiate. And last but not least, bonus tip. Don't overpack your schedule. Marrakesh is a sensory overload. Leave some gaps to wander. sit at cafes or escape to a quiet garden. You'll enjoy the city much more if you don't rush from one side to another. And we've got some great places to recommend. So stay tuned for upcoming Marrakesh series. And if you're wondering about day trips from Marrakesh, be sure to check out our published vlogs. We had an amazing time at the Atlas Studios in Adid Benhadu as well as our day trip to Esawea. Check out these vlogs on our channel. And that's it. These were our insights to help you arrive in Marrakesh feeling prepared, confident, and ready to dive into Morocco's magic. Moreover, we hope these insights help make your trip planning and first moments in the city a lot easier and a whole lot more enjoyable. If you like this vlog and you found it useful, go ahead and give it a thumbs up. It really helps our channel grow. Plus, if you have any other questions about arriving in Morocco or if you've already been and you want to share tips of your own, then go ahead and add them in the comment section below. We love to hear from you. Be sure to subscribe if you haven't yet because in next week's vlog we'll be diving deeper into Marrakesh, discovering rooftop cafes, hidden gems, gardens, and so much more. Thanks for watching everyone. Shukran lim mushahadi. Catch you in the medina next.

videos.transcript: Welcome to Marrakesh, the vibrant heart of Morocco. In this video, I'll guide you through how to make the most of your time in this enchanting city. From where to stay and how to get around to must-sea places, and unforgettable day trips, I'll cover you everything you need to know for an amazing experience. Get ready for an adventure through the bustling souks, stunning palaces, lush gardens, and of course, the delicious Moroccan cuisine. Let's dive in and make your visit to Marrakesh truly [Music] unforgettable. We arrive at Marakekesh Manara Airport on a late morning in March. Upon arrival, we recommend you two things. Exchange some money for Moroccan dirhams and buy a SIM card. You'll need cash for taxes since credit cards in Morocco are rarely accepted and a SIM card will help you call one. Keep in mind that prices at the airport are much higher. A SIM card with internet data cost €20 here while in the city center is just €10. The exchange rate at the airport is also less favorable, so only exchange a small amount of money. Alternatively, you can withdraw money from the ATMs, but bear in mind that they charge a €7 fee. For taxis, download and use the in drive app is cheaper than calling one on the street. The airport is pretty close to the city center, and the ride to the medina shouldn't cost more than €810. For accommodation, if you're willing to spend at least €100 per night, we recommend staying in a riyad in the medina, the old town of Marakekesh. However, if you're looking to save some money, staying just outside the Medina is a great option as a taxi ride to the old town will only cost you around €45, which is quite affordable. We stayed at Victoria Apartments in the trendy elites neighborhood, paying just €65 per night for a high standard apartment. An excellent deal compared to the lower quality and noise you would find in the city center for the same price. We start our exploration of the city by visiting what is probably the number one attraction in Marrakesh, Leardin Majorel. Created in 1923 by French painter Jacqu Majorel, the garden is a stunning blend of vibrant colors and exotic plant life. Major who was passionate about plants and their beauty designed the garden as a peaceful retreat. The iconic blue color known as majoral blue is one of the standout features of the garden. In 1980, the property was bought by Giza Laurent and his partner Pierre B who lovely restored it and open it to the public. Today it remains a serene escape from the bustling streets, noise and pollution of Marrakesh, offering a peaceful oasis that is much needed in this vibrant city. The entrance fee is around €16 and it's important to note that you must buy ticket in advance either through official website or through the website getyouguide.com. I leave you the link in the video description. You'll be asked to select a specific time for your visit. But once inside, you can stay for as long as you like. Be sure to book at least a few days in advance as tickets can sell out quickly. Next to Jardim Majorel, you'll find the Isaloran Museum, which celebrates the legendary designer's work and his strong connection to Morocco. We didn't visit the museum ourselves as it wasn't our main interest, but it's definitely a mustsea for fashion enthusiasts. You can also purchase a combo ticket that grants access to both the museum and the Major Gardens. Check the link in the description for more details. In the late afternoon, we headed to explore two of Marrakesh most iconic landmarks, the Kotubia Mosque and Jima Elfna, the pulsing heart of the city. The Kotubia mosque, standing tall with its striking 77 m mined is one of the most important landmarks in Marrakesh. Built in the late 12th century, is a magnificent example of Islamic architecture. Although non-Muslims can't enter the mosque, the stunning exterior and surrounding gardens are well worth the visit. From there, we headed to Jima Alfna, the vibrant main square that captures the sense of Marrakesh. As evening falls, the squares come alive with street performers, snake charmers, musicians, and food stalls creating an exciting chaotic atmosphere. [Music] On day two, we visited Madrasa Benuzf, one of Marakekesh top attractions. Built in the 14th century during the Sadian dynasty, it was once the largest Islamic school in North Africa, offering religious education to hundreds of students. The Madrasa is known for its stunning architecture, including intricate tile work, carved wood, and ornate stuckco. Inside you'll find a tranquil courtyard with a central fontain surrounded by beautifully decorated rooms. The upper floors provide a great view of the courtyard and its stunning craftsmanship. The entrance fee is 50 dirhams around €5 payable in cash. We recommend visiting early in the morning to avoid the crowds and fully enjoy its peaceful beauty. One place I highly recommend is the Moroccan Tea House Restaurant 112 located right next to the Madrasa. It's a peaceful oasis in the heart of the medina surrounded by palm trees and lush greenery. The tea house offers a variety of tea options and delicious meals. Moreover, there is also a small museum that showcases Moroccan tea culture. Another place nearby I highly recommend is the restaurant Lafen Dy Peace. We stopped here for lunch and we were extremely satisfied with the food service and the amazing views from the rooftop terrace. The atmosphere was perfect and we paid around €50 for two main dishes, drinks and dessert. It's a great spot for a relaxed meal with a beautiful view of a medina. After lunch, we headed to Leardin Secret, the secret garden, which was also visible from the terrace of a restaurant. This beautiful garden, originally built in the 16th century, is a peaceful retreat showcasing traditional Islamic architecture and lush greenery. The entrance fee is around €10. Next, we made our way to the southern part of the medina to visit some of its main attraction, Bajia Palace, Elbadi Palace, and the Sadian Toms. The entrance fee for each of these places is €10. Unfortunately, due to the early closures for Ramadan period, we didn't have a chance to visit these sites, but here's what we read. Bajia Palace built by a powerful court official who served the sultan is a 19th century palace which showcases Morocco's finest palace architecture with its stunning courtyards and gardens. Elbadi Palace built in the late 16th century by Sultan Ahmad El Mansour was once a grand palace known for its vast courtyards and reflecting pools although much of it it was dismantled later on. The Sydian tombs housing members of the Sydian dynasty are impressive, but only two small areas are open to the public with long cues and limited viewing time. We then visited the Tim Smith Square before exploring the Marrakesh souks. The souks of Marrakesh are a vibrant maze of narrow streets bustling with energy and offering an incredible variety of goods. Spread throughout the city, these traditional markets are each specialized in different products, making every visit an exploration of Moroccan culture. We dedicated day three to exploring Esawira easily the best day trip from Marrakesh. Located around 2 hour and a half by bus away, Isawira is a charming coastal city founded in the late 18th century and briefly served as Morocco's capital. The city is known for its historic medina, stunning beaches, vibrant art scene, and fresh seafood. I've made a specific video about the Sawira where I go more into details. Find link in the video description along with the booking details for the day trip through the website getyougu.com. On the morning of day four, we visited a mustsea spot, Bacha Coffee, a cafe museum that's truly one of a kind. Built in the early 20th century, Bacha Coffee is considered one of the oldest and most iconic cafes in Marrakesh. It was established to offer a luxurious space where the lead could enjoy fine coffee in an elegant setting. To get a table at the bar, be prepared to wait in long cues. The best way to avoid this is to arrive at 9:00 a.m. an hour before it opens. The complex is made up of four main areas: the bar, the coffee shop, the courtyard, and the museum. Access to the first three areas cost just €1, but if you want to visit the museum, you'll need an additional ticket, which cost €6. A coffee here cost around €5, and we ended up spending €20 for two coffees and two croissants. While it may seem pricey, I must say it was worth it for the experience. We dedicated the afternoon to an excursion to the Agafai Desert, a stunning desert landscape just outside Marakeesh. We boo our trip again through getyougu.com and the program included hotel pickup quad and camel rides and a dinner under the stars with a dance and fire show. I'll go into more detail about this experience in a separate video. You'll find the link to watch the video and to book this highly recommended organized tour in the video description. If you have enjoyed this video, please give it a thumbs up and consider subscribing to the channel. Your support means the world to us and help us bring you even more exciting travel adventures. Thank you so much for being part of this amazing journey. [Music] [Applause] [Music]
